keyAchievements.description: His ideas on secularism, populism, and nationalism provided the direct intellectual framework for many of Mustafa Kemal Atatürk's foundational reforms.

keyAchievements.title: Distinction between Culture and Civilization
keyAchievements.description: He developed a key sociological concept distinguishing 'culture' (national and spiritual) from 'civilization' (international and material), guiding Turkey's path to modernization.

faq.question: Was Ziya Gökalp religious?
faq.answer: While he was a strong advocate for a secular state where religion was separate from politics and law, he viewed Islam as a vital and inseparable component of Turkish national culture and identity.

faq.question: What was his relationship with Atatürk?
faq.answer: Gökalp was a major intellectual influence on Atatürk. Atatürk adopted and implemented many of Gökalp's ideas on nationalism, secularism, and modernization when establishing the Republic of Turkey.

faq.question: What is 'Turkism' according to Gökalp?
faq.answer: Turkism, as defined by Gökalp, is an ideology promoting the cultural and political solidarity of Turkish peoples. It emphasizes a shared language, history, and culture—not race—as the foundation of the nation.

timeline.event: Born in the village of Pari, near modern-day Malayer, to the Zand tribe.
Nader Shah is assassinated, plunging Iran into civil war. Karim Khan returns to his homeland and begins to consolidate power.
Becomes a major contender for power in western Iran after forming a tripartite alliance.
Establishes Shiraz as his capital after defeating all major rivals and controlling most of Iran. He adopts the title Vakil ol-Ro'aya (Regent of the People).
His reign is marked by peace and prosperity. He commissions major construction projects in Shiraz, including the Arg of Karim Khan, Vakil Bazaar, and Vakil Mosque.
Dies of natural causes in Shiraz. His death triggers succession struggles that lead to the downfall of the Zand dynasty.

keyAchievements.title: یکپارچه‌سازی ایران
keyAchievements.description: He reunified most of Iran after the chaotic period of civil war that followed Nader Shah's death.

keyAchievements.title: Era of Peace and Prosperity
keyAchievements.description: His nearly 30-year reign is remembered as a period of exceptional calm, justice, and economic growth for the Iranian people.

keyAchievements.title: Patronage of Arts and Architecture
keyAchievements.description: He transformed his capital, Shiraz, with magnificent architectural projects and fostered a flourishing of arts and culture.

faq.question: Why did he refuse the title of 'Shah' (King)?
faq.answer: He preferred the title 'Vakil ol-Ro'aya' (Regent of the People) to show that his power derived from and was exercised on behalf of his subjects, emphasizing a more humble and just form of rule.

faq.question: What is his most famous building?
faq.answer: The Arg of Karim Khan, a large citadel located in the center of Shiraz, which served as his living quarters and military command center.

faq.question: How did the Zand dynasty end?
faq.answer: After Karim Khan's death, his incompetent and feuding successors weakened the state, allowing Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar to conquer their territories and establish the Qajar dynasty.

timeline.event: Born in Constantinople (modern-day Istanbul).
Appointed as a clerk in the imperial army's Anatolian financial bureaucracy.
Participated in Ottoman campaigns against the Safavids in Persia, traveling to Baghdad and Hamadan.
Inherited a large fortune, which allowed him to leave his bureaucratic post and pursue a life of scholarship.
Began compiling his monumental bibliographic work, 'Kashf al-Zunun' (The Removal of Doubt from the Names of Books and Arts).
Began writing his major geographical work, 'Jihannuma' (Mirror of the World).
Died in Constantinople, leaving several of his major works to be completed by others.

keyAchievements.title: Kashf al-Zunun
keyAchievements.description: Authored a massive bibliographic encyclopedia listing over 14,500 books and 9,500 authors in Arabic, Persian, and Turkish.

keyAchievements.title: Jihannuma
keyAchievements.description: Wrote a groundbreaking universal geography that integrated Islamic and European sources, a first in the Ottoman world.

keyAchievements.title: Fezleke
keyAchievements.description: Compiled a comprehensive chronicle of Ottoman history from 1592 to 1655, serving as a key historical source.

keyAchievements.title: Mizan al-Haqq
keyAchievements.description: Wrote 'The Balance of Truth,' a collection of essays on controversial religious and ethical topics, advocating for a more rational approach.

faq.question: Why is he called Katip Çelebi?
faq.answer: 'Katip' means 'scribe' or 'clerk,' reflecting his early career in the Ottoman bureaucracy. 'Çelebi' was an honorific title meaning 'gentleman' or 'man of God.' His real name was Mustafa ibn Abdullah.

faq.question: What makes his work 'Kashf al-Zunun' so important?
faq.answer: It is one of the most comprehensive bibliographic works of the pre-modern era, providing invaluable information on thousands of books and authors, many of which are now lost or rare.

faq.question: Was Katip Çelebi only a historian?
faq.answer: No, he was a true polymath. He wrote extensively on geography, bibliography, ethics, religion, and science, attempting to synthesize all available knowledge of his time.

faq.question: How did his military service influence his work?
faq.answer: His travels during military campaigns exposed him to different regions, cultures, and libraries across the empire. This firsthand experience, combined with the loss he witnessed, fueled his desire to preserve and organize knowledge for future generations.

timeline.event: Born to King Darius I and Atossa, a daughter of Cyrus the Great.
Ascends to the throne of the Achaemenid Empire after the death of his father, Darius I.
Crushes a major revolt in Egypt, asserting his authority.
Violently suppresses a revolt in Babylon, confiscating the sacred statue of Marduk.
Launches a massive invasion of Greece. Wins the Battle of Thermopylae, sacks Athens, but suffers a decisive naval defeat at the Battle of Salamis.
The Persian army left in Greece under his general Mardonius is decisively defeated at the Battle of Plataea, ending the invasion.
Focuses on ambitious construction projects, notably at Persepolis and Susa, completing many buildings his father had started.
Assassinated in a palace conspiracy led by Artabanus, the commander of his royal bodyguard.

keyAchievements.title: Suppression of Revolts
keyAchievements.description: Successfully quelled major rebellions in Egypt and Babylon early in his reign, securing the stability of the empire.

keyAchievements.title: Invasion of Greece
keyAchievements.description: Assembled one of the largest multinational armies of the ancient world for his campaign against the Greek city-states.

keyAchievements.title: Grand Construction Projects
keyAchievements.description: Completed many of his father's building projects and initiated his own, significantly expanding the magnificent city of Persepolis.

faq.question: Why is Xerxes often portrayed as a villain or a tyrant?
faq.answer: Most surviving historical accounts of his invasion of Greece were written by the victorious Greeks (like Herodotus), who naturally depicted him as a tyrannical and hubristic antagonist to glorify their own victory.

faq.question: Did Xerxes really have a bridge of boats built across the Hellespont?
faq.answer: Yes, this is a well-documented engineering feat. His engineers yoked together hundreds of ships to create two pontoon bridges, allowing his massive army to cross from Asia into Europe.

faq.question: What was Xerxes's religion?
faq.answer: Xerxes was a follower of Zoroastrianism, the state religion of the Achaemenid Empire, which centered on the worship of a single supreme god, Ahura Mazda. He references Ahura Mazda in his royal inscriptions.

faq.question: How did Xerxes die?
faq.answer: He was assassinated in 465 BCE in his bedchamber as part of a palace conspiracy. The plot was likely orchestrated by Artabanus, the commander of his bodyguard, who then briefly held power before being overthrown by Xerxes's son, Artaxerxes I.

timeline.event: Born in Harran, Upper Mesopotamia (modern-day Turkey).
Encounters Muhammad ibn Musa ibn Shakir, who invites him to Baghdad to work as a scholar.
Joins the circle of scholars in Baghdad, working under the patronage of the Banu Musa and later the Caliph al-Mu'tadid.
Produces major translations of Greek works (Euclid's Elements, Ptolemy's Almagest) and original treatises on mathematics, astronomy, and mechanics.
Dies in Baghdad, leaving a legacy carried on by his son, Sinan, and grandson, Ibrahim.

keyAchievements.title: Translation and Preservation of Greek Science
keyAchievements.description: Translated foundational Greek texts by Euclid, Archimedes, Apollonius, and Ptolemy into Arabic, making them accessible to the Islamic world.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Trepidation
keyAchievements.description: Proposed a theory of the oscillation of the equinoxes to account for perceived variations in the precession rate.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Number Theory
keyAchievements.description: Discovered a theorem for finding pairs of amicable numbers, extending the work of the ancient Greeks.

keyAchievements.title: Foundations of Statics
keyAchievements.description: Authored 'Kitab fi'l-qarastun' (The Book of the Steelyard), a fundamental work on mechanics and the principles of levers and equilibrium.

faq.question: What religion did Thabit ibn Qurra follow?
faq.answer: He was a member of the Sabian religious sect from Harran, a Hellenistic pagan group that revered stars and celestial bodies. This often caused friction with orthodox Muslim authorities.

faq.question: What are amicable numbers?
faq.answer: Amicable numbers are two different numbers so related that the sum of the proper divisors of each is equal to the other number. Thabit's rule is a generalization that helped find such pairs.

faq.question: Was his theory of trepidation correct?
faq.answer: No, the theory of trepidation was later superseded by a more accurate understanding of uniform precession. However, it was an important and influential attempt to explain observational discrepancies of his time.

timeline.event: Born Fátimih Baraghání in Qazvin, Persia.
Becomes one of the first followers of the Báb, recognized as the 17th Letter of the Living.
Attends the Conference of Badasht, where she removes her veil, symbolizing a break with the past.
Placed under house arrest due to her influential teachings.
Executed by strangulation in a garden in Tehran for her Bábí faith.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering Feminist Icon
keyAchievements.description: Became one of the first major advocates for women's rights in the Middle East, challenging religious and social norms.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Bábí Theologian
keyAchievements.description: Was the only woman designated as a 'Letter of the Living,' the Báb's first disciples, shaping early Bábí doctrine.

keyAchievements.title: Influential Poet
keyAchievements.description: Authored powerful mystical and revolutionary poetry that continues to inspire readers and activists today.

faq.question: Why is she called Tahirih?
faq.answer: The title 'Tahirih' (meaning 'The Pure One') was bestowed upon her at the Conference of Badasht, recognizing her spiritual station and purity of spirit. Sources attribute the bestowing of the title to either the Báb or Baháʼu'lláh.

faq.question: Did she ever meet the Báb in person?
faq.answer: No, she never met the Báb. Their entire connection was through letters and the accounts of other followers, yet she became one of his most prominent disciples.

faq.question: What was the significance of her unveiling?
faq.answer: In 19th-century Persia, a woman appearing unveiled before men was a radical and shocking act. It symbolized a deliberate break from Islamic law and tradition, heralding the new spiritual and social laws of the Bábí dispensation, which included greater equality between men and women.

timeline.event: Born as Muhammad bin Suleyman, likely in the region of modern-day Iraq (Karbala or Hillah).
Receives a comprehensive education, mastering Azerbaijani, Persian, and Arabic, along with theology and mathematics.
The Ottoman Empire under Suleiman the Magnificent captures Baghdad from the Safavids, shifting the political landscape of Fuzûlî's life.
Composes his masterpiece, the romantic epic 'Leylâ vü Mecnûn' (Layla and Majnun) in Azerbaijani Turkic.
Writes the famous 'Şikâyetnâme' (The Book of Complaint), a satirical prose work protesting his not receiving a promised government stipend.
Dies during a plague outbreak in Karbala, where he was serving as a custodian at the shrine of Imam Husayn.

keyAchievements.title: Divans in Three Languages
keyAchievements.description: He composed extensive collections of poetry (divans) in Azerbaijani, Persian, and Arabic, showcasing his unparalleled linguistic and poetic mastery.

keyAchievements.title: Leylâ vü Mecnûn
keyAchievements.description: He authored the definitive Azerbaijani version of the classic Middle Eastern love story, which became a cornerstone of Turkic literature.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of the Azerbaijani Literary Language
keyAchievements.description: He elevated the Azerbaijani Turkic language to a high literary standard, profoundly influencing generations of poets across the Turkic world.

faq.question: Why did he choose the pen name 'Fuzûlî'?
faq.answer: He chose it because he believed no one else would want it, ensuring his uniqueness. The name has multiple meanings, including 'impertinent' or 'superfluous,' but can also imply a higher, virtuous quality, reflecting his complex artistic persona.

faq.question: What is his most famous work?
faq.answer: His most celebrated work is 'Leylâ vü Mecnûn' (Layla and Majnun), a poetic epic that tells the tragic story of a love that transcends the material world, blending earthly passion with Sufi mysticism.

faq.question: Was Fuzûlî a Sufi?
faq.answer: While not formally initiated into a specific Sufi order, his poetry is deeply infused with Sufi concepts, such as the annihilation of the self in the divine (fana), the pain of separation from the Beloved (God), and the use of love and wine as metaphors for spiritual ecstasy.

timeline.event: Born in Paj, a village near Tus in Khorasan, Sasanian Empire (modern-day Iran).
Begins composing the Shahnameh, his life's work, to preserve the history and mythology of Persia.
Completes the first version of the Shahnameh and presents it to Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni, who famously underpays him.
Dies in his hometown of Tus, reportedly in poverty, but leaving behind a work that would define Persian culture.

keyAchievements.title: Authoring the Shahnameh
keyAchievements.description: He created the 'Shahnameh' (Book of Kings), the world's longest epic poem by a single author, which is central to Persian culture and identity.

keyAchievements.title: Revitalization of the Persian Language
keyAchievements.description: His near-exclusive use of Persian in the Shahnameh played a crucial role in reviving and standardizing the language after centuries of Arabic dominance.

faq.question: Why is the Shahnameh so important?
faq.answer: It is the national epic of Greater Iran, preserving its myths, pre-Islamic history, and language. It's considered a masterpiece of world literature and a cornerstone of Persian identity.

faq.question: Was Ferdowsi wealthy?
faq.answer: No. Despite his noble birth, he spent his family's fortune on his work and, according to legend, died in poverty after being slighted by his patron, Sultan Mahmud.

faq.question: How long is the Shahnameh?
faq.answer: It consists of over 50,000 rhyming couplets (distichs), making it more than twice the length of Homer's Iliad and Odyssey combined.

timeline.event: Born Ahmed Muhiddin Piri in Gallipoli, Ottoman Empire.
Begins his seafaring career, sailing with his uncle, the corsair and admiral Kemal Reis.
Compiles his first world map, famously known as the Piri Reis map, and presents it to Sultan Selim I.
Completes his 'Kitab-ı Bahriye' (Book of Navigation), a detailed atlas of the Mediterranean.
Creates his second world map, of which only a fragment showing Central and North America survives.
Appointed as the Reis (Admiral) of the Ottoman Fleet in the Indian Ocean.
Executed in Cairo on charges of treason and bribery following the siege of Hormuz.

keyAchievements.title: The Piri Reis Map of 1513
keyAchievements.description: One of the oldest surviving world maps to show the Americas, known for its remarkable detail and mysterious sources.

keyAchievements.title: Kitab-ı Bahriye (Book of Navigation)
keyAchievements.description: A comprehensive nautical atlas of the Mediterranean, containing detailed charts, port information, and sailing directions.

faq.question: How accurate is the Piri Reis map?
faq.answer: The map is remarkably accurate for its time regarding the coasts of Europe and North Africa. Its depiction of the Americas and a supposed southern continent (Antarctica) is a subject of intense debate and speculation among historians and cartographers.

faq.question: Why was Piri Reis executed?
faq.answer: He was executed on charges of treason and bribery after lifting the siege of the Portuguese-held Hormuz and returning to Egypt with his fleet, an act which his political enemies framed as desertion and corruption.

faq.question: What does 'Reis' mean?
faq.answer: 'Reis' is a Turkish title, equivalent to a captain, master, or admiral. Piri Reis's given name was Ahmed Muhiddin Piri.

timeline.event: Born in Rayy (in modern-day Iran).
Becomes the fifth Abbasid Caliph after the death of his brother al-Hadi.
Establishes or greatly expands the House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) in Baghdad.
Launches a major campaign against the Byzantine Empire, reaching the Bosporus.
تقسیم جانشینی امپراتوری میان پسرانش، امین و مأمون، که زمینه را برای درگیری‌های آینده فراهم کرد.
دستور بازداشت خاندان قدرتمند برمکی، و پایان دادن به نفوذ آنها بر دربار.
درگذشت در طوس (خراسان، ایران امروزی) در حین لشکرکشی برای سرکوب یک شورش.

keyAchievements.title: حمایت از بیت‌الحکمه
keyAchievements.description: تأسیس «بیت‌الحکمه» در بغداد، کتابخانه و موسسه ترجمه بزرگی که به قلب دوران طلایی اسلام تبدیل شد.

keyAchievements.title: شکوفایی علمی و فرهنگی
keyAchievements.description: دوران حکومت او شاهد پیشرفت‌های بی‌سابقه‌ای در علم، پزشکی، ریاضیات و فلسفه بود که دانشمندان را از سراسر جهان شناخته‌شده به خود جذب کرد.

keyAchievements.title: روابط دیپلماتیک با شارلمانی
keyAchievements.description: آغاز یکی از نخستین تبادلات دیپلماتیک بزرگ میان جهان اسلام و اروپای غربی، از جمله هدیه معروف فیل به نام ابوالعباس.

faq.question: آیا هارون‌الرشید واقعاً شبیه شخصیتش در داستان‌های «هزار و یک شب» بود؟
faq.answer: داستان‌ها تخیلی و رمانتیک شده‌اند. در حالی که من به گشت‌وگذار با لباس مبدل در خیابان‌های بغداد برای آگاهی از احوال مردمم معروف بودم، اما ماجراجویی‌های جادویی، مخلوقات ادبی هستند که بازتاب‌دهنده تجمل دوران من می‌باشند.

faq.question: بیت‌الحکمه چه بود؟
faq.answer: این مرکز علمی بزرگی در دوران سلطنت من در بغداد بود. کار خود را به عنوان کتابخانه شخصی من آغاز کرد اما به یک فرهنگستان عمومی و مرکز ترجمه تبدیل شد که در آن متون یونانی، فارسی و هندی به عربی ترجمه می‌شدند و دانش را حفظ کرده و گسترش می‌دادند.

faq.question: چرا خاندان برمکی از قدرت سقوط کردند؟
faq.answer: برمکیان، وزیران مورد اعتماد من، قدرت و ثروت عظیمی به دست آورده بودند. سقوط ناگهانی آنها در سال ۸۰۳ احتمالاً به دلیل تمایل من به تحکیم قدرت خود و محدود کردن نفوذ آنها بود، هرچند دلایل دقیق آن همچنان موضوع بحث‌های تاریخی است.

timeline.event: تولد شمس‌الدین محمد حافظ شیرازی در شیراز، پارس.
تصرف شیراز توسط سلسله آل مظفر، که بر جایگاه حافظ و حمایت‌های مالی دربار از او تأثیر گذاشت.
شاه شجاع، حامی شعر، پس از دوره‌ای از بی‌مهری، حافظ را به جایگاهش در دربار بازمی‌گرداند.
افتادن دوباره از چشم دربار و احتمال ورود به دوره‌ای از تبعید از شیراز.
فتح شیراز توسط تیمور لنگ. ملاقاتی افسانه‌ای، هرچند به احتمال زیاد ساختگی، میان شاعر و جهانگشا رخ می‌دهد.
درگذشت در شیراز و خاکسپاری در باغ‌های مصلی. آرامگاه او امروزه یک بنای ملی و زیارتگاه است.

keyAchievements.title: دیوان حافظ
keyAchievements.description: مجموعه اشعار او، به ویژه در قالب غزل، اوج ادبیات فارسی و یک معیار فرهنگی به شمار می‌رود.

keyAchievements.title: استادی در غزل
keyAchievements.description: او قالب شعر غنایی فارسی یعنی غزل را به کمال رساند و آن را با عمق عرفانی و لایه‌های معنایی بی‌سابقه‌ای درآمیخت.

keyAchievements.title: نماد فرهنگی ماندگار
keyAchievements.description: شعر او برای تفأل (فال حافظ) استفاده می‌شود و در فرهنگ، آموزش و هویت ایرانی جایگاهی محوری دارد.

faq.question: معنی نام «حافظ» چیست؟
faq.answer: نامی هنری به معنای «حفظ‌کننده» یا «نگهبان» است که به طور سنتی به کسی داده می‌شود که کل قرآن را از حفظ کرده باشد.

faq.question: آیا شعر او درباره عشق زمینی است یا عشق الهی؟
faq.answer: این موضوع به طرز معروفی دوپهلو است. واژه‌هایی مانند «معشوق»، «می» (شراب) و «خرابات» (میکده) هم به عنوان لذت‌های زمینی و مادی و هم به عنوان نمادهای عرفانی صوفیانه برای خدا، خلسه معنوی و مکان عبادت حقیقی تفسیر می‌شوند.

faq.question: فال حافظ چیست؟
faq.answer: یک سنت تفأل است که در آن ایرانیان به دنبال راهنمایی یا بصیرت، نیتی می‌کنند و سپس دیوان حافظ را به طور تصادفی می‌گشایند و شعر آمده را به عنوان پاسخ تفسیر می‌نمایند.

faq.question: آیا حافظ واقعاً با تیمور جهانگشا ملاقات کرد؟
faq.answer: افسانه معروفی درباره ملاقات آنها وجود دارد که در آن حافظ به طرز هوشمندانه‌ای به انتقاد تیمور از شعرش پاسخ می‌دهد. با اینکه داستان تاثیرگذاری است، اما بیشتر مورخان آن را ساختگی می‌دانند.

timeline.event: Born in Constantinople, Ottoman Empire.
Graduated from the American College for Girls in Constantinople.
Divorced her first husband, Salih Zeki, over his decision to take a second wife.
Delivered her famous speech at the Sultanahmet demonstration against the Allied occupation.
Fled to Anatolia to join the Turkish National Movement; served in the Turkish War of Independence.
Lived in self-imposed exile in Europe and the United States due to political disagreements with the new government.
Returned to Turkey after Atatürk's death.
Elected as a member of the Grand National Assembly of Turkey.
Died in Istanbul.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneering Turkish Novelist
keyAchievements.description: Authored influential novels like "The Shirt of Flame" and "The Clown and His Daughter," which explored themes of national identity and women's roles.

keyAchievements.title: Hero of the Turkish War of Independence
keyAchievements.description: Served as a corporal and sergeant on the front lines, becoming a powerful symbol of national resistance.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Feminist and Activist
keyAchievements.description: Was one of the first graduates of her college and a lifelong advocate for women's education and rights in Turkey.

keyAchievements.title: Member of Parliament
keyAchievements.description: Was elected to the Turkish Parliament in 1950, continuing her public service in the republic she helped create.

faq.question: Why is she considered a feminist icon?
faq.answer: She divorced her husband for attempting polygamy, advocated for women's education, and her novels featured strong, independent female characters, challenging the patriarchal norms of her time.

faq.question: Did she really fight as a soldier?
faq.answer: Yes. During the Turkish War of Independence, she was officially enlisted and served with the ranks of corporal and later sergeant, primarily in administrative and medical support roles near the front lines.

faq.question: Why did she go into exile after helping found the Republic?
faq.answer: She and her husband disagreed with what they saw as the increasingly authoritarian direction of Mustafa Kemal Atatürk's government. They chose self-imposed exile to avoid political persecution.

timeline.event: Born in Babylon.
Ascends to the throne of Babylon after the abdication of his father, Sin-Muballit.
Conquers the cities of Isin and Uruk.
Defeats a coalition of enemies and conquers the powerful kingdom of Larsa, significantly expanding his empire.
Conquers the kingdom of Mari, securing control over most of Mesopotamia.
Establishes the Code of Hammurabi on a diorite stele, which is publicly displayed in Babylon.
Dies after a reign of 42 years, and is succeeded by his son, Samsu-iluna.

keyAchievements.title: Code of Hammurabi
keyAchievements.description: Created one of the world's earliest and most complete sets of written laws, establishing principles of justice and order for his empire.

keyAchievements.title: Unification of Mesopotamia
keyAchievements.description: Transformed Babylon from a minor city-state into a dominant empire controlling the fertile crescent through military conquest and diplomacy.

keyAchievements.title: Public Works and Infrastructure
keyAchievements.description: Oversaw major projects including the expansion of canals for irrigation and the fortification of city walls, boosting agriculture and security.

faq.question: Was 'an eye for an eye' applied to everyone equally in the Code of Hammurabi?
faq.answer: No, the punishments varied based on the social status of the perpetrator and the victim. A crime against a nobleman received a harsher penalty than the same crime against a commoner or a slave.

faq.question: Was Hammurabi the first king to write down laws?
faq.answer: No, earlier law codes like the Code of Ur-Nammu existed. However, Hammurabi's code was far more comprehensive, systematic, and well-preserved, making it the most famous from the ancient Near East.

faq.question: What happened to Hammurabi's empire after his death?
faq.answer: The empire began to decline under his successor, Samsu-iluna. It faced internal revolts and external pressure, eventually shrinking and falling to invaders, but Hammurabi's cultural and legal legacy endured for centuries.

timeline.event: Born as Aleksandra Lisowska in Ruthenia, Kingdom of Poland (modern-day Ukraine).
Captured by Crimean Tatars and brought to Constantinople, entering the Imperial Harem of Sultan Suleiman.
Gives birth to her first son, Şehzade Mehmed, solidifying her status in the harem.
Suleiman frees and legally marries Hurrem, making her the Haseki Sultan, an unprecedented event.
Her rival, Grand Vizier Pargalı Ibrahim Pasha, is executed. Many historians believe she influenced this decision.
Begins construction of the Haseki Sultan Complex in Constantinople, a major charitable foundation.
Suleiman's firstborn son, Şehzade Mustafa, is executed on suspicion of treason. Hurrem is widely seen as having orchestrated his downfall to secure the throne for her own sons.
Dies in the Topkapi Palace and is buried in a mausoleum next to Suleiman's at the Süleymaniye Mosque.

keyAchievements.title: Breaking Imperial Tradition
keyAchievements.description: Became the first imperial consort to be legally wedded to a Sultan, shattering centuries of Ottoman custom.

keyAchievements.title: Political Advisor and Diplomat
keyAchievements.description: Acted as Sultan Suleiman's chief political advisor and managed diplomatic relations with foreign powers, including Poland and Persia.

keyAchievements.title: Architectural Patronage
keyAchievements.description: Commissioned numerous major public buildings, including the Haseki Sultan Complex, which provided services to the community.

keyAchievements.title: Ushering in the 'Sultanate of Women'
keyAchievements.description: Her unprecedented power established a new era where imperial women exerted extraordinary political influence.

faq.question: What do the names 'Roxelana' and 'Hurrem' mean?
faq.answer: 'Roxelana' was a European nickname meaning 'the Ruthenian one,' referring to her origins. 'Hurrem' was the name given to her by Suleiman, from the Persian 'Khurram,' which means 'the cheerful one.'

faq.question: Why was her marriage to Suleiman so significant?
faq.answer: For over a century, Ottoman sultans had not officially married, instead relying on concubines to produce heirs. Her marriage elevated her status above all other women and gave her unprecedented security and legitimacy.

faq.question: Was she really as ruthless as she is often portrayed?
faq.answer: She was a shrewd political operator who removed rivals to protect her and her children's future. While the extent of her 'ruthlessness' is debated, her actions were taken within the brutal context of succession politics where the price of failure was often death.

faq.question: How did she influence the succession to the throne?
faq.answer: By allegedly engineering the downfall of Suleiman's most capable sons from other women (Mustafa and Bayezid), she cleared the path for her less-capable son, Selim II, to become Sultan, an event some historians argue marked the beginning of the Empire's decline.

timeline.event: Born Thomas Edward Lawrence in Tremadog, Wales.
Studies Modern History at Jesus College, Oxford, and undertakes a walking tour of Syria to research crusader castles for his thesis.
World War I begins; he is assigned to the geographical section of the General Staff in London and later to military intelligence in Cairo.
Joins the Arab Revolt as a British liaison officer to the forces of Emir Faisal.
Leads a small Arab force in a daring desert march to capture the strategic Ottoman-held port of Aqaba.
Participates in the capture of Damascus, a key victory for the Allied and Arab forces.
Serves as an advisor to Emir Faisal's Arab delegation at the Paris Peace Conference, where he is disillusioned by the outcome.
Enlists in the Royal Air Force under the assumed name John Hume Ross to escape his fame.
Publishes a private, subscribers-only edition of his epic war memoir, 'Seven Pillars of Wisdom'.
Dies at the age of 46 from injuries sustained in a motorcycle accident in Dorset, England.

keyAchievements.title: Role in the Arab Revolt
keyAchievements.description: Played a crucial role as a military strategist and liaison, uniting disparate Arab tribes against the Ottoman Empire during WWI.

keyAchievements.title: Capture of Aqaba
keyAchievements.description: Led a daring desert march to capture the key port of Aqaba from its undefended landward side, a major strategic victory.

keyAchievements.title: Seven Pillars of Wisdom
keyAchievements.description: Authored an epic and introspective memoir of the war that is considered a classic of English literature and military history.

faq.question: Was 'Lawrence of Arabia' his real name?
faq.answer: No, his name was Thomas Edward Lawrence. 'Lawrence of Arabia' was a nickname popularized by American journalist Lowell Thomas, whose articles and lectures made Lawrence famous worldwide.

faq.question: Why did he join the military under a fake name after the war?
faq.answer: He sought to escape the pressures and scrutiny of his fame. He craved anonymity and the structured, mechanical life of an ordinary serviceman.

faq.question: Did he succeed in securing Arab independence?
faq.answer: While the revolt was a military success, he was ultimately disillusioned by the Sykes-Picot Agreement, a secret pact between Britain and France that divided the Middle East into spheres of influence, betraying promises of a unified Arab state.

faq.question: What is 'Seven Pillars of Wisdom' about?
faq.answer: It is his detailed and highly personal account of his experiences during the Arab Revolt. It's a complex work blending military history, adventure, and deep psychological introspection.
